title: A.B., Harvard College
subtitle: Social Relations
description: An interdisciplinary beginning that set the frame for a career linking developmental science, public systems, and social policy.

title: M.S., Yale University
subtitle: Clinical-Community Psychology
description: Clinical and community psychology begin to merge into a developmental and intervention-oriented perspective.

title: Ph.D., Yale University
subtitle: Clinical-Community and Developmental Psychology
description: Formal consolidation of the developmental lens that would later shape work on poverty, violence, and policy.

title: Barnard College and Columbia Graduate Faculties
subtitle: Assistant to Associate Professor; Director, Barnard Center for Toddler Development
description: The early academic era: toddlers, attachment, maltreatment, and the developmental consequences of adversity.

title: Attachment and toddler socio-emotional development
subtitle: Longitudinal work on parent-child relationships
description: Research on early competencies, parental representations, and how early relational patterns organize later development.

title: Maltreatment and developmental psychopathology
subtitle: Developmental sequelae of child abuse and neglect
description: A major line of work tracing how violence, neglect, and risk reshape trajectories of cognition, emotion, and adaptation.

title: Children and armed conflict
subtitle: Comparative work in Lebanon and beyond
description: A shift toward war, trauma, and the psychosocial development of children living through conflict.

title: Columbia University
subtitle: Director, National Center for Children in Poverty
description: A policy-forward era tying child development to neighborhood poverty, labor markets, welfare reform, and public systems.

title: Neighborhood Poverty volumes
subtitle: A landmark synthesis with Brooks-Gunn and Duncan
description: The neighborhood books anchor a major period of research on place, inequality, and child development.

title: School climate and social-emotional learning
subtitle: From Resolving Conflict Creatively to 4Rs
description: Violence prevention evolves into a broader school-based agenda around SEL, literacy, classroom quality, and translational science.

title: New York University
subtitle: Professor of Applied Psychology and Public Policy; later Distinguished Professor and University Professor
description: NYU becomes the long-running base for scaling intervention science, policy work, and global partnerships.

title: Fellow, Association for Psychological Science
subtitle: Recognition of broad scholarly influence
description: A marker of how far the work had traveled across developmental science, policy, and intervention research.

title: Global TIES and international field trials
subtitle: Ghana, DRC, South Africa, Lebanon, Niger
description: The scholarship becomes decisively global, with cluster-randomized trials and implementation research across conflict- and poverty-affected settings.

title: University Professor, NYU
subtitle: Interdisciplinary platform for research and policy
description: An institutional role matching the breadth of the work: developmental science, social policy, and humanitarian learning systems.

title: Beyond Survival
subtitle: National Academies / global early childhood agenda
description: A highly visible expression of the global policy turn: investing in young children worldwide, especially in fragile conditions.

title: Child poverty reduction and policy roadmap work
subtitle: Public-facing research and national policy engagement
description: The later period returns to poverty reduction with renewed urgency, connecting deep research programs to public policy design.

title: Paulette Goddard Professor of Psychology and Public Policy
subtitle: NYU
description: The present chapter: a capstone appointment spanning decades of work across developmental science and social intervention.
